title: My grandmother's hands
summary: A grandchild watches her grandmother's hands all day. The hands cook, sew, feed chickens, draw, and tell stories.
body:
My grandmother is eighty. Her hands are old. They are small. They have many lines. The skin is soft, like paper. I like her hands.

She uses her hands all day. In the morning, she makes rice. Her hands wash the rice three times. Then they cook it in a pot. The hands know the time. They do not need a timer.

At ten, she sews. She has a small needle. The needle moves fast. Her eyes are not good now, but her hands remember. She fixes my shirt. She fixes a hole in my dad's pocket. She fixes a button on my coat.

At lunch, her hands feed the chickens. The chickens come to her. They are not afraid. They know her hands.

In the afternoon, she draws with me. We sit at the table. Her hands hold a pencil. The pencil moves slowly. She draws a flower. I draw a flower too. Her flower is more beautiful.

At night, she tells me stories. Her hands move when she talks. The hands tell part of the story. The mouth tells the other part. I listen to both.

I am eight. My hands are smooth. One day, my hands will be like hers. I will be glad.

annotations.meaningVi: vui mừng, hạnh phúc (cụm cố định)

grammarNotes.title: Third person singular: she + verb-s
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Khi nói về một người ở ngôi thứ ba số ít (she/he/it), động từ thêm 's' hoặc 'es'. Trong bài: 'She uses her hands all day.' / 'She sews.' / 'She draws a flower.' Lưu ý: 'fix' → 'fixes', 'draw' → 'draws'.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: With third-person singular (she/he/it), add 's' or 'es' to the verb. From the story: 'She uses her hands all day.' / 'She sews.' / 'She draws a flower.' Note: 'fix' → 'fixes', 'draw' → 'draws'.

blanks.explanation: Old hands have many lines on them.
She sews with a small needle.
Her eyes are weak now, but her hands remember the work.
She fixes a hole in her son's pocket.
Her hands hold a pencil to draw.
At night she tells the child stories.
Young hands are smooth — without lines.

translationVi:
Bà tôi tám mươi tuổi. Bàn tay bà đã già. Chúng nhỏ. Chúng có nhiều đường nhăn. Da mềm như giấy. Tôi thích bàn tay bà.

Bà dùng bàn tay cả ngày. Buổi sáng, bà nấu cơm. Bàn tay bà vo gạo ba lần. Rồi chúng nấu cơm trong một cái nồi. Bàn tay biết thời gian. Chúng không cần đồng hồ hẹn giờ.

Lúc mười giờ, bà khâu. Bà có một cây kim nhỏ. Kim đi nhanh. Mắt bà giờ không tốt nữa, nhưng bàn tay nhớ. Bà vá áo cho tôi. Bà vá một lỗ ở túi áo bố tôi. Bà đính lại một cái cúc trên áo khoác của tôi.

Lúc trưa, bàn tay bà cho gà ăn. Lũ gà đến với bà. Chúng không sợ. Chúng biết bàn tay bà.

Buổi chiều, bà vẽ cùng tôi. Chúng tôi ngồi ở bàn. Bàn tay bà cầm bút chì. Bút chì đi chậm. Bà vẽ một bông hoa. Tôi cũng vẽ một bông hoa. Hoa của bà đẹp hơn.

Buổi tối, bà kể chuyện cho tôi. Bàn tay bà động đậy khi bà nói. Bàn tay kể một phần của câu chuyện. Miệng kể phần khác. Tôi nghe cả hai.

Tôi tám tuổi. Bàn tay tôi còn mượt. Một ngày, bàn tay tôi sẽ như bàn tay bà. Tôi sẽ vui.

questions.prompt: What does the writer compare her grandmother's skin to?
What does she make in the morning?
How many times do her hands wash the rice?
questions.explanation: 'Her hands wash the rice three times.'

questions.prompt: Why can her hands still sew when her eyes are weak?
options: Because she has a friend who helps
questions.explanation: 'Her eyes are not good now, but her hands remember.'

questions.prompt: What does she fix on the writer's coat?
questions.explanation: 'She fixes a button on my coat.'

questions.prompt: Why do the chickens come to her hands at lunch?
options: They are not afraid — they know her hands
questions.explanation: 'The chickens come to her. They are not afraid. They know her hands.'

questions.prompt: What do the grandmother and the writer draw together?
questions.explanation: 'She draws a flower. I draw a flower too.'

questions.prompt: When the grandmother tells stories at night, what do her hands do?
options: They move and tell part of the story
questions.explanation: 'Her hands move when she talks. The hands tell part of the story.'

questions.prompt: How does the writer feel about her own hands becoming like her grandmother's?
questions.explanation: 'One day, my hands will be like hers. I will be glad.'

criticalThinkingQuestion: The writer says her grandmother's hands 'know the time' and 'remember' the work without her eyes. Think of a small task in your life that your hands or body know without you thinking. How did they learn?
